tr.beam-deflection-formula-explained:
Servis limitleri çoğu zaman akma limitinden önce devreye girer (L/360 vb.).

Euler-Bernoulli formülleri AluCalc kiriş motorunda kullanılır.

I ve E seçimi sehime en çok etki eden parametrelerdir.
tr.bearing-life-calculation-explained:
L10 ömrü ISO 281'e göre %90 güvenilirlikte %10 arıza oranını tanımlar.

(C/P)^p ilişkisi AluCalc rulman çekirdeğiyle birebir aynıdır — topu yuvarlak için p=3.

P > C durumu kritik uyarıdır: eşdeger yük katalog derecesini aşıyor.
tr.chip-breaker-logic:
CNC tornada talaş kontrolü yüzey kalitesi ve takım ömrünü belirler.

Asimetrik uç ve radyal/ flanksal besleme kesme kuvvetini %15–21 azaltabilir.

TQ geometrileri diş açmada talaş kırılmasını optimize eder.
tr.engineering-units-and-standards:
Mühendislik nicel bir disiplindir. Standartlaştırılmış birimler olmadan küresel üretim ve iş birliği mümkün olmazdı. Bu modülde Uluslararası Birim Sistemi (SI) ve İngiliz sistemini inceliyoruz.

Modern tedarik zincirleri kıtaları kapsar. SI uluslararası referanstır; İngiliz birimleri ABD havacılık ve eski makinelerde hâlâ kullanılır.

Bu ders AluCalc dönüşüm disiplinini kurar: kesin sabitler, tutarlı anlamlı rakamlar ve her ara sonuçta açık birim etiketleri.
tr.fundamentals-of-statics:
Statik, dengede kalan sistemleri inceler. Kuvvet ve moment etkileşimini anlamak tüm yapısal analiz ve makine tasarımının temelidir.

Her makine gövdesi, bina mesneti ve kaldırma düzeneği eleman boyutlandırmadan önce dengeyi sağlamalıdır. Statik şekil değişimini ihmal eder — uygulanan yükler altında cisim hareketsiz kalır mı sorusunu yanıtlar.

Kafes Analizi motorunun kullandığı ΣF = 0 ve ΣM = 0 mantığını burada öğrenirsiniz: cismi izole edin, tüm dış etkileri hesaba katın, bilinmeyen tepkileri çözün.
tr.how-to-calculate-bolt-torque:
Cıvata torku montajın kritik girdisidir; tasarım çıktısı ön yüktür (clamp force).

T = K·F·d kısa yolu atölyede kullanılır; VDI 2230 tam sürtünme modelini uygular.

Aşırı tork akma veya diş yırtılmasına, yetersiz tork ise gevşemeye neden olur.
tr.introduction-to-machine-elements:
Makineler standart yapı taşlarından oluşur: birleştirme, güç aktarımı, destekleme ve sızdırmazlık.

Bu modül cıvata, rulman, dişli ve kayış ailelerini AluCalc hesaplayıcılarına bağlar — her biri ISO/DIN tabanlıdır.

Özel parça icat etmeden önce katalog elemanı seçmeyi öğrenirsiniz; bu endüstriyel tasarımın verimlilik ilkesidir.
tr.mechanics-of-materials-fundamentals:
Statik dış kuvvetleri verir; malzeme mukavemeti iç direnci inceler.

σ = F/A ve ε = σ/E ilişkisi tüm yapısal kontrollerin temelidir.

Elastik sınırın ötesi plastik bölgeye girer — bu ders lineer elastik aralıkta kalır.
tr.mohrs-circle-stress-analysis:
Mohr çemberi düzlem gerilme durumlarında asal gerilmeleri ve τ_max değerini bulur.

σ₁, σ₂ ve kayma gerilmesi yorulma ve akma kontrollerinde kullanılır.

AluCalc Mohr Circle Lab aynı geometrik inşayı interaktif uygular.
tr.motor-power-calculation:
Motor boyutlandırmada P = T·n/9550 (kW) endüstride standarttır.

Aktarım kayıplarını (η) hesaba katmadan seçilen motor yetersiz kalır.

Bu ders güç aktarımı zincirinde motor, kayış ve mil boyutlandırmasını birbirine bağlar.
tr.pressure-drop-calculation-guide:
Darcy-Weisbach boru kayıplarını hesaplar: ΔP = f·(L/D)·(ρV²/2).

Pompa basınç başlığı ve debi boyutlandırmasının temelidir.

Reynolds sayısı akış rejimini (laminer/türbülans) belirler.
tr.thread-geometry-standards:
Diş standartları helis geometrisini kodlar. M16×1,5-6g bir tornacı için çap, adım ve tolerans bandını tanımlar.

Profil açısı uyumsuzluğu (60° vs 55°) montajda diş yırtılmasına yol açar.

Bu ders Thread Geometry motoru ile VDI 2230 cıvata motoru arasındaki ortak parametreleri hizalar.
tr.torsion-and-buckling-mechanics:
Mil burulma τ = T·r/J ile; kolon burkulma Euler formülü P_cr = π²EI/(KL)² ile analiz edilir.

Burkulma ani ve tehlikelidir — gerilme limitinin altında bile oluşabilir.

Efektif uzunluk K·L seçimi mesnet koşullarına bağlıdır.

de.beam-deflection-formula-explained:
Structural integrity relies on controlling deformation. Deflection is the displacement of a structural element under load, and calculating it is critical for ensuring serviceability and safety in engineering design.

Strength alone does not make a good design — users feel deflection. Serviceability limits like L/360 for floors and L/250 for crane rails often govern before stress reaches yield. Euler–Bernoulli beam theory gives closed-form δ for standard supports.

The Beam Deflection Engine in AluCalc uses the same PL³/(48EI) backbone for simply supported center loads.
de.bearing-life-calculation-explained:
Predicting the operational lifespan of a rolling-element bearing is vital for maintenance planning and machine reliability. The L10 life represents the life that 90% of a group of identical bearings will achieve or exceed before the first sign of fatigue develops.

Bearings fail by rolling-contact fatigue long before static strength limits are reached. ISO 281 defines dynamic load rating C and life L10 — the revolutions at which 10% of bearings fail under equivalent load P at 90% reliability.

This lesson teaches the same (C/P)^p relationship implemented in the AluCalc ISO 281 Bearing Engine.
de.chip-breaker-logic:
In high-speed CNC threading, the chip is your enemy. If it doesn't break, it nests around the tool, destroying the surface finish and the insert. Modern TQ geometries solve this with asymmetric pressure points. This course breaks down the fluid-like behavior of metal during the cut.

CNC threading is a controlled metal-cutting process. Chip form determines whether you get short broken chips or a bird's nest wrapped around the tool. TQ geometries use asymmetric points to steer chip flow and cut cutting forces by 15–21%.

This lesson connects Kyocera threading data to the Machining Time engine in AluCalc.
de.engineering-units-and-standards:
Engineering is a quantitative discipline. Without standardized units, global collaboration and manufacturing would be impossible. In this module, we explore the International System of Units (SI) and the Imperial system, focusing on how to convert between them accurately.

Modern supply chains span continents — a drawing in millimeters must mate with tooling calibrated in inches. The SI system (meter, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela) is the international reference, while Imperial units persist in US aerospace and legacy machinery.

This lesson establishes the conversion discipline used throughout AluCalc: exact constants, consistent significant figures, and explicit unit labels on every intermediate result.
de.fundamentals-of-statics:
Statics is the branch of mechanics that deals with physical systems in equilibrium. Understanding how forces and moments interact is the foundation for all structural analysis and machine design.

Every machine frame, building support, and lifting rig must satisfy equilibrium before you size members. Statics ignores deformation — it answers whether the body remains at rest under applied loads.

You will use the same ΣF = 0 and ΣM = 0 logic that powers the Truss Analysis engine: isolate the body, account for every external action, and solve for unknown reactions.
de.how-to-calculate-bolt-torque:
Bolt torque calculation is one of the most critical aspects of mechanical assembly. Incorrect torque leads to joint failure, loosening, or even catastrophic structural damage. In real-world engineering, torque must be calculated using preload force, friction coefficient, and bolt diameter — not guesswork.

Clamp force — not torque — is the design output of a bolted joint. Torque is merely the shop-floor input, distorted by friction at the threads and under the head. VDI 2230 formalizes the relationship between torque, preload, and thread utilization.

This lesson connects handbook shortcuts (T = K·F·d) to the full friction model in the AluCalc Fastener Engine.
de.introduction-to-machine-elements:
Machines are composed of standard building blocks called Machine Elements. From the simplest screw to complex planetary gears, understanding these components is the first step toward becoming a machine designer.

Designers rarely invent new parts from scratch. They select standardized machine elements — bolts, bearings, gears, belts, shafts, keys, and seals — and compose them into functional assemblies. Each element family has catalog ratings, failure modes, and ISO/DIN standards.

This overview maps those families to the specialized academy lessons and AluCalc calculators you will use for quantitative sizing.
de.mechanics-of-materials-fundamentals:
While Statics deals with external forces, Mechanics of Materials looks inside. How do materials respond to those forces? Do they stretch, bend, or break? This course defines the internal resistance of solid bodies to external loads.

Once reactions are known from statics, you must ask whether the material survives. Normal stress σ = F/A and Hooke's law ε = σ/E link force to deformation. Stress concentrations at holes and fillets can locally exceed nominal values.

Every strength calculator in AluCalc — from fasteners to beams — builds on this σ–ε foundation.
de.mohrs-circle-stress-analysis:
In multi-axial loading, the stress at a point depends on the orientation of the plane you are looking at. Mohr's Circle is a powerful graphical method (and mathematical model) to find the maximum and minimum stresses a material will ever see.

Real components rarely see pure axial stress. Bending, torsion, and press fits combine into plane-stress states (σx, σy, τxy). Mohr's circle transforms these components to principal stresses and maximum shear — the values used in ductile failure criteria.

The Principal Stress Simulator in this lesson mirrors the analytical kernel in AluCalc Mohr Circle Lab.
de.motor-power-calculation:
Sizing an electric motor requires understanding the dynamic relationship between rotational speed and the torque required to overcome load resistance and friction.

Electric motors are sized on power, but loads present as torque at a speed. The bridge equation P = T·n/9550 (kW) appears in every drive catalog. Gearboxes, belts, and couplings add efficiency losses that must be added back to the motor nameplate.

This lesson prepares you for belt-drive and motor sizing tools in AluCalc where the same torque–speed–power triangle applies.
de.pressure-drop-calculation-guide:
Fluid transport requires energy to overcome internal friction. Pressure drop calculation is the cornerstone of pump sizing and piping system design.

Pumps are sized on flow and head — head includes friction losses in pipe, fittings, and valves. Darcy–Weisbach relates pressure drop to friction factor, geometry, and velocity head. Reynolds number selects laminar vs turbulent correlations.

The Fluid Flow engine in AluCalc evaluates the same ΔP = f·(L/D)·ρV²/2 relationship for line sizing.
de.thread-geometry-standards:
Every bolt is a complex mathematical helix. To the untrained eye, they look similar, but the difference between a 60° Metric thread and a 55° Whitworth thread is the difference between a secure joint and a catastrophic failure. This module decodes the geometric standards used in modern CNC machining.

Thread standards encode helix geometry in a compact designation — M16×1.5-6g tells a machinist the nominal diameter, pitch, and tolerance band. CNC threading cycles, tap drill charts, and fastener strength calculations all depend on reading these symbols correctly.

This module aligns shop-floor vocabulary with the Thread Geometry engine in AluCalc, covering metric, UN, Whitworth, and trapezoidal families.
de.torsion-and-buckling-mechanics:
Not all failures are caused by direct stress. Torsion causes shafts to twist and shear, while Buckling causes slender columns to suddenly collapse under loads they would otherwise carry. Understanding these two phenomena is critical for machine and structural stability.

Shafts fail in shear from torsion; slender columns fail in buckling at loads far below material yield. Euler's Pcr = π²EI/(KL)² depends on end conditions through K and always uses the minimum moment of inertia.

The Column Buckling engine in AluCalc applies this same stability check with your actual boundary conditions.

In real-world engineering, torque must be calculated using preload force, friction coefficient, and bolt diameter — not guesswork.

Clamp force — not torque — is the design output of a bolted joint. Torque is merely the shop-floor input, distorted by friction at the threads and under the head. VDI 2230 formalizes the relationship between torque, preload, and thread utilization.

This lesson connects handbook shortcuts (T = K·F·d) to the full friction model in the AluCalc Fastener Engine.
it.introduction-to-machine-elements:
Machines are composed of standard building blocks called Machine Elements. From the simplest screw to complex planetary gears, understanding these components is the first step toward becoming a machine designer.

Designers rarely invent new parts from scratch. They select standardized machine elements — bolts, bearings, gears, belts, shafts, keys, and seals — and compose them into functional assemblies. Each element family has catalog ratings, failure modes, and ISO/DIN standards.

This overview maps those families to the specialized academy lessons and AluCalc calculators you will use for quantitative sizing.
it.mechanics-of-materials-fundamentals:
While Statics deals with external forces, Mechanics of Materials looks inside. How do materials respond to those forces? Do they stretch, bend, or break? This course defines the internal resistance of solid bodies to external loads.

Once reactions are known from statics, you must ask whether the material survives. Normal stress σ = F/A and Hooke's law ε = σ/E link force to deformation. Stress concentrations at holes and fillets can locally exceed nominal values.

Every strength calculator in AluCalc — from fasteners to beams — builds on this σ–ε foundation.
it.mohrs-circle-stress-analysis:
In multi-axial loading, the stress at a point depends on the orientation of the plane you are looking at. Mohr's Circle is a powerful graphical method (and mathematical model) to find the maximum and minimum stresses a material will ever see.

Real components rarely see pure axial stress. Bending, torsion, and press fits combine into plane-stress states (σx, σy, τxy). Mohr's circle transforms these components to principal stresses and maximum shear — the values used in ductile failure criteria.

The Principal Stress Simulator in this lesson mirrors the analytical kernel in AluCalc Mohr Circle Lab.
it.motor-power-calculation:
Sizing an electric motor requires understanding the dynamic relationship between rotational speed and the torque required to overcome load resistance and friction.

Electric motors are sized on power, but loads present as torque at a speed. The bridge equation P = T·n/9550 (kW) appears in every drive catalog. Gearboxes, belts, and couplings add efficiency losses that must be added back to the motor nameplate.

This lesson prepares you for belt-drive and motor sizing tools in AluCalc where the same torque–speed–power triangle applies.
it.pressure-drop-calculation-guide:
Fluid transport requires energy to overcome internal friction. Pressure drop calculation is the cornerstone of pump sizing and piping system design.

Pumps are sized on flow and head — head includes friction losses in pipe, fittings, and valves. Darcy–Weisbach relates pressure drop to friction factor, geometry, and velocity head. Reynolds number selects laminar vs turbulent correlations.

The Fluid Flow engine in AluCalc evaluates the same ΔP = f·(L/D)·ρV²/2 relationship for line sizing.
it.thread-geometry-standards:
Every bolt is a complex mathematical helix. To the untrained eye, they look similar, but the difference between a 60° Metric thread and a 55° Whitworth thread is the difference between a secure joint and a catastrophic failure. This module decodes the geometric standards used in modern CNC machining.

Thread standards encode helix geometry in a compact designation — M16×1.5-6g tells a machinist the nominal diameter, pitch, and tolerance band. CNC threading cycles, tap drill charts, and fastener strength calculations all depend on reading these symbols correctly.

This module aligns shop-floor vocabulary with the Thread Geometry engine in AluCalc, covering metric, UN, Whitworth, and trapezoidal families.
it.torsion-and-buckling-mechanics:
Not all failures are caused by direct stress. Torsion causes shafts to twist and shear, while Buckling causes slender columns to suddenly collapse under loads they would otherwise carry. Understanding these two phenomena is critical for machine and structural stability.

Shafts fail in shear from torsion; slender columns fail in buckling at loads far below material yield. Euler's Pcr = π²EI/(KL)² depends on end conditions through K and always uses the minimum moment of inertia.

The Column Buckling engine in AluCalc applies this same stability check with your actual boundary conditions.

pt.beam-deflection-formula-explained:
Structural integrity relies on controlling deformation. Deflection is the displacement of a structural element under load, and calculating it is critical for ensuring serviceability and safety in engineering design.

Strength alone does not make a good design — users feel deflection. Serviceability limits like L/360 for floors and L/250 for crane rails often govern before stress reaches yield. Euler–Bernoulli beam theory gives closed-form δ for standard supports.

The Beam Deflection Engine in AluCalc uses the same PL³/(48EI) backbone for simply supported center loads.
pt.bearing-life-calculation-explained:
Predicting the operational lifespan of a rolling-element bearing is vital for maintenance planning and machine reliability. The L10 life represents the life that 90% of a group of identical bearings will achieve or exceed before the first sign of fatigue develops.

Bearings fail by rolling-contact fatigue long before static strength limits are reached. ISO 281 defines dynamic load rating C and life L10 — the revolutions at which 10% of bearings fail under equivalent load P at 90% reliability.

This lesson teaches the same (C/P)^p relationship implemented in the AluCalc ISO 281 Bearing Engine.
pt.chip-breaker-logic:
In high-speed CNC threading, the chip is your enemy. If it doesn't break, it nests around the tool, destroying the surface finish and the insert. Modern TQ geometries solve this with asymmetric pressure points. This course breaks down the fluid-like behavior of metal during the cut.

CNC threading is a controlled metal-cutting process. Chip form determines whether you get short broken chips or a bird's nest wrapped around the tool. TQ geometries use asymmetric points to steer chip flow and cut cutting forces by 15–21%.

This lesson connects Kyocera threading data to the Machining Time engine in AluCalc.
pt.engineering-units-and-standards:
Engineering is a quantitative discipline. Without standardized units, global collaboration and manufacturing would be impossible. In this module, we explore the International System of Units (SI) and the Imperial system, focusing on how to convert between them accurately.

Modern supply chains span continents — a drawing in millimeters must mate with tooling calibrated in inches. The SI system (meter, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela) is the international reference, while Imperial units persist in US aerospace and legacy machinery.

This lesson establishes the conversion discipline used throughout AluCalc: exact constants, consistent significant figures, and explicit unit labels on every intermediate result.
pt.fundamentals-of-statics:
Statics is the branch of mechanics that deals with physical systems in equilibrium. Understanding how forces and moments interact is the foundation for all structural analysis and machine design.

Every machine frame, building support, and lifting rig must satisfy equilibrium before you size members. Statics ignores deformation — it answers whether the body remains at rest under applied loads.

You will use the same ΣF = 0 and ΣM = 0 logic that powers the Truss Analysis engine: isolate the body, account for every external action, and solve for unknown reactions.

This lesson prepares you for belt-drive and motor sizing tools in AluCalc where the same torque–speed–power triangle applies.
ja.pressure-drop-calculation-guide:
Fluid transport requires energy to overcome internal friction. Pressure drop calculation is the cornerstone of pump sizing and piping system design.

Pumps are sized on flow and head — head includes friction losses in pipe, fittings, and valves. Darcy–Weisbach relates pressure drop to friction factor, geometry, and velocity head. Reynolds number selects laminar vs turbulent correlations.

The Fluid Flow engine in AluCalc evaluates the same ΔP = f·(L/D)·ρV²/2 relationship for line sizing.
ja.thread-geometry-standards:
Every bolt is a complex mathematical helix. To the untrained eye, they look similar, but the difference between a 60° Metric thread and a 55° Whitworth thread is the difference between a secure joint and a catastrophic failure. This module decodes the geometric standards used in modern CNC machining.

Thread standards encode helix geometry in a compact designation — M16×1.5-6g tells a machinist the nominal diameter, pitch, and tolerance band. CNC threading cycles, tap drill charts, and fastener strength calculations all depend on reading these symbols correctly.

This module aligns shop-floor vocabulary with the Thread Geometry engine in AluCalc, covering metric, UN, Whitworth, and trapezoidal families.
ja.torsion-and-buckling-mechanics:
Not all failures are caused by direct stress. Torsion causes shafts to twist and shear, while Buckling causes slender columns to suddenly collapse under loads they would otherwise carry. Understanding these two phenomena is critical for machine and structural stability.

Shafts fail in shear from torsion; slender columns fail in buckling at loads far below material yield. Euler's Pcr = π²EI/(KL)² depends on end conditions through K and always uses the minimum moment of inertia.

The Column Buckling engine in AluCalc applies this same stability check with your actual boundary conditions.

ko.beam-deflection-formula-explained:
Structural integrity relies on controlling deformation. Deflection is the displacement of a structural element under load, and calculating it is critical for ensuring serviceability and safety in engineering design.

Strength alone does not make a good design — users feel deflection. Serviceability limits like L/360 for floors and L/250 for crane rails often govern before stress reaches yield. Euler–Bernoulli beam theory gives closed-form δ for standard supports.

The Beam Deflection Engine in AluCalc uses the same PL³/(48EI) backbone for simply supported center loads.
ko.bearing-life-calculation-explained:
Predicting the operational lifespan of a rolling-element bearing is vital for maintenance planning and machine reliability. The L10 life represents the life that 90% of a group of identical bearings will achieve or exceed before the first sign of fatigue develops.

Bearings fail by rolling-contact fatigue long before static strength limits are reached. ISO 281 defines dynamic load rating C and life L10 — the revolutions at which 10% of bearings fail under equivalent load P at 90% reliability.

This lesson teaches the same (C/P)^p relationship implemented in the AluCalc ISO 281 Bearing Engine.
ko.chip-breaker-logic:
In high-speed CNC threading, the chip is your enemy. If it doesn't break, it nests around the tool, destroying the surface finish and the insert. Modern TQ geometries solve this with asymmetric pressure points. This course breaks down the fluid-like behavior of metal during the cut.

CNC threading is a controlled metal-cutting process. Chip form determines whether you get short broken chips or a bird's nest wrapped around the tool. TQ geometries use asymmetric points to steer chip flow and cut cutting forces by 15–21%.

This lesson connects Kyocera threading data to the Machining Time engine in AluCalc.
ko.engineering-units-and-standards:
Engineering is a quantitative discipline. Without standardized units, global collaboration and manufacturing would be impossible. In this module, we explore the International System of Units (SI) and the Imperial system, focusing on how to convert between them accurately.

Modern supply chains span continents — a drawing in millimeters must mate with tooling calibrated in inches. The SI system (meter, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela) is the international reference, while Imperial units persist in US aerospace and legacy machinery.

This lesson establishes the conversion discipline used throughout AluCalc: exact constants, consistent significant figures, and explicit unit labels on every intermediate result.
ko.fundamentals-of-statics:
Statics is the branch of mechanics that deals with physical systems in equilibrium. Understanding how forces and moments interact is the foundation for all structural analysis and machine design.

Every machine frame, building support, and lifting rig must satisfy equilibrium before you size members. Statics ignores deformation — it answers whether the body remains at rest under applied loads.

You will use the same ΣF = 0 and ΣM = 0 logic that powers the Truss Analysis engine: isolate the body, account for every external action, and solve for unknown reactions.
ko.how-to-calculate-bolt-torque:
Bolt torque calculation is one of the most critical aspects of mechanical assembly. Incorrect torque leads to joint failure, loosening, or even catastrophic structural damage. In real-world engineering, torque must be calculated using preload force, friction coefficient, and bolt diameter — not guesswork.

Clamp force — not torque — is the design output of a bolted joint. Torque is merely the shop-floor input, distorted by friction at the threads and under the head. VDI 2230 formalizes the relationship between torque, preload, and thread utilization.

This lesson connects handbook shortcuts (T = K·F·d) to the full friction model in the AluCalc Fastener Engine.
ko.introduction-to-machine-elements:
Machines are composed of standard building blocks called Machine Elements. From the simplest screw to complex planetary gears, understanding these components is the first step toward becoming a machine designer.

Designers rarely invent new parts from scratch. They select standardized machine elements — bolts, bearings, gears, belts, shafts, keys, and seals — and compose them into functional assemblies. Each element family has catalog ratings, failure modes, and ISO/DIN standards.

This overview maps those families to the specialized academy lessons and AluCalc calculators you will use for quantitative sizing.
ko.mechanics-of-materials-fundamentals:
While Statics deals with external forces, Mechanics of Materials looks inside. How do materials respond to those forces? Do they stretch, bend, or break? This course defines the internal resistance of solid bodies to external loads.

Once reactions are known from statics, you must ask whether the material survives. Normal stress σ = F/A and Hooke's law ε = σ/E link force to deformation. Stress concentrations at holes and fillets can locally exceed nominal values.

Every strength calculator in AluCalc — from fasteners to beams — builds on this σ–ε foundation.
ko.mohrs-circle-stress-analysis:
In multi-axial loading, the stress at a point depends on the orientation of the plane you are looking at. Mohr's Circle is a powerful graphical method (and mathematical model) to find the maximum and minimum stresses a material will ever see.

Real components rarely see pure axial stress. Bending, torsion, and press fits combine into plane-stress states (σx, σy, τxy). Mohr's circle transforms these components to principal stresses and maximum shear — the values used in ductile failure criteria.

The Principal Stress Simulator in this lesson mirrors the analytical kernel in AluCalc Mohr Circle Lab.
